behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is real filesystem watching semantics (recursive vs non-recursive watch behavior) plus daemon wiring. A fake event emitter can miss OS/library-specific subdirectory event behavior, so mocking can hide real failures.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file into that subdirectory, and assert one transformed output appears in the output directory for that nested file.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is primarily deterministic state/time-window logic for one path key. It can be exercised with injected time and repeated requests without real OS/network/subprocess. Rejected higher levels: the text focuses on 'deduplication logic' and does not require real filesystem event behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the dedup component (or nearest single boundary) twice with the same file path using a controlled clock: first request accepted, second within window rejected, third after window accepted.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: If dedup is not isolated in code, raise to Integration; as written, the minimum for this behavior is Unit.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior text explicitly depends on OS event emission patterns. Duplicate-event burst timing and watcher-library behavior are real infrastructure effects that in-process fakes can misrepresent, so System is the minimum to catch true failures.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon on a real filesystem, perform a single file write pattern known to generate multiple change events (e.g., temp-write + rename or chunked write), and assert exactly one processing/output record for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is an OS-level watcher lifecycle condition (directory deletion/recreation) that requires real filesystem events and watch re-registration behavior. Mocking the watcher can hide real rebind failures.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real directory, delete that directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and verify it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real filesystem timestamp metadata and concurrent arrival behavior. In-process mocks can force idealized timestamps/order and miss real granularity and ordering edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files nearly simultaneously on a real watched directory with controlled creation timing, capture processing order from daemon logs/output, and assert it matches ascending creation timestamp ordering.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: If implementation reads synthetic timestamps from an internal queue rather than filesystem metadata, Integration could be enough; behavior wording specifies creation timestamps, so System remains minimum.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This is a real filesystem race between write completion and watcher arrival events. Partial-write visibility, rename patterns, and file readiness are OS/file API behaviors that mocks often hide.
behaviors.test_description: Begin a slow/chunked write to a file in the watched directory, trigger arrival early, and verify daemon does not process until the writer closes/completes; then assert exactly one successful post-completion processing result.
